title: Empire biscuits
subtitle: Scottish shortbread sandwiched with jam and finished with glacé icing
excerpt: Two rounds of crisp shortbread sandwiched with raspberry jam, coated in a thick white glacé icing, and finished with half a glacé cherry. A Scottish teatime standard. Makes about sixteen sandwiched biscuits.
sourceNotes: The empire biscuit is a Scottish Lowland and Border tradition documented in Scottish household baking manuals of the late Victorian and Edwardian period. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: jam-sandwiched iced biscuits appear in the confectionery and biscuit entries alongside shortbread rounds. The glacé-iced, cherry-topped form is the shape recorded across twentieth-century Scottish bakery guides and community cookbooks, where it appears alongside the fly cemetery and the Glasgow macaroon as an everyday bakery item.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the baked biscuits before icing and assembly. Ice from frozen; the icing sets faster.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly. Roll the second batch while the first bakes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The biscuit dough can be wrapped and refrigerated for up to 48 hours.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 170°C in a conventional oven

glossaryTerms.definition: A simple icing made from icing sugar and a small amount of liquid, stirred to a thick, smooth paste. It flows enough to coat evenly but sets firm as it dries. Use water for a neutral finish, or replace with lemon juice or milk for flavour. Sift the icing sugar first to avoid lumps.

content.text: Empire biscuits are a bakery item made at home: the kind of thing that appears on a plate at Scottish afternoon tea without fanfare. The biscuit itself is plain shortbread. The jam is sharp enough to cut through the sweetness of the icing. The glacé cherry on top is not optional.

Preheat the oven to 160°C fan. Combine the {{plain-flour}} and {{icing-sugar}} (biscuit amount) in a large bowl. Rub in the cold {{unsalted-butter}} until the mixture resembles fine breadcrumbs. Press together until a smooth dough forms. Wrap and chill for 30 minutes.

On a lightly floured surface, roll the dough to 4–5 mm thick. Cut into rounds using a 6 cm cutter; you need an even number. Arrange on a lined baking tray and bake for 18–20 minutes, until pale gold at the edges and set across the surface. Cool completely on a rack before assembling.

Spread a thin layer of {{jam}} on the flat side of half the biscuits and press the remaining rounds on top. Stir the {{icing-sugar}} (icing amount) into the {{whole-milk}} or water a little at a time until you have a thick, smooth

that just holds its shape but flows when the spoon is lifted. Spoon a little icing onto each biscuit and spread to the edges with the back of the spoon. Press half a {{glace-cherries}} into the centre of each. Leave at room temperature for 30 minutes until the icing is fully set.
